moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the Grünfeld Defense, a hypermodern opening where Black allows White to build a massive center and then systematically destroys it. White plays 1.d4, claiming the center. The Grünfeld leads to sharp, concrete positions where Black's pressure on d4 is relentless.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Single central pawn on d4; White plans to expand with c4.
plans: Build a broad pawn center with c4 and e4
Develop pieces behind the central pawns
alternatives: e4 — leading to more open, tactical positions

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays 1...Nf6, the Indian approach. The Nf6 controls e4 and d5, contesting central control with a piece rather than a pawn.
plans: Control e4 to prevent White from achieving the full center easily
Keep options open for the central pawn structure
alternatives: d5 — the classical response, immediately contesting the center

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays c4, expanding the central control. The c4+d4 duo grabs maximum space, and White is preparing for the full e4 center.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White pawns on c4 and d4 form the broad center, controlling d5 and e5.
plans: Add e4 to build the dream center
Develop Nc3 to reinforce the central pawns
alternatives: Nf3 — a more restrained approach

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays g6, signaling the Grünfeld setup. The bishop will go to g7 where it becomes the centerpiece of Black's strategy — pressuring d4 along the entire a1-h8 diagonal. Unlike the KID (where Black plays ...d6 and ...e5), the Grünfeld's ...d5 immediately challenges the center.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black delays central pawn play, opting for piece pressure against White's center instead.
plans: Fianchetto the bishop to g7 targeting d4
Strike the center with d5 at the right moment
alternatives: e6 — entering Nimzo/QGD territory

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays Nc3, defending c4 and preparing e4. The Nc3 controls d5 and is a key piece in maintaining the central advantage.
plans: Push e4 to build the full pawn center
Complete development and castle
alternatives: Nf3 — developing the other knight first, more flexible

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strikes with d5, the defining Grünfeld move. Rather than the KID's ...d6, Black immediately challenges the center head-on. After cxd5 Nxd5, Black will exchange the knight and put maximum pressure on d4 with the Bg7 — the Grünfeld's core strategy.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Central tension: d5 vs c4/d4. Black invites White to capture and build a big center that becomes a.
plans: After cxd5 Nxd5, sacrifice the center to attack it with pieces
Use the g7 bishop as a long-range weapon against d4
alternatives: Bg7 — completing the fianchetto first, entering King's Indian territory

moveAnnotations.annotation: White captures cxd5, accepting the central exchange. This is the most principled response — White aims for e4 with a powerful broad center.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: After Nxd5, White will play e4 gaining a huge center.
plans: Build the big center with e4
Use the center as a launchpad for attacking chances
alternatives: Nf3 — keeping the tension, a positional approach

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black recaptures Nxd5, placing the knight centrally. But this is temporary — the plan is to exchange the knight on c3, then pressure d4 with the Bg7, ...c5, and ...Nc6.
plans: Allow e4, then retreat the knight via Nxc3 to damage White's structure
Complete the fianchetto and target d4
alternatives: Qxd5 — rarely played, the queen is exposed

moveAnnotations.annotation: White pushes e4, establishing the dream center with pawns on d4 and e4. This massive formation controls c5, d5, e5, and f5. The Grünfeld's entire philosophy is that this center is a TARGET, not a strength — Black will spend the game attacking it.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's ideal center: d4+e4. Impressive but potentially vulnerable to piece pressure and pawn brea.
plans: Maintain the center and use it for a kingside attack
Develop pieces to support d4 and e4
alternatives: Nf3 — a more modest approach keeping a flexible center

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black exchanges Nxc3, the key Grünfeld idea. By trading the knight for White's Nc3, Black damages the center's support structure — the c3 pawn can no longer support d4, and the b-pawn is doubled. The Bg7 now has a clear target on d4.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: After bxc3, White has doubled c-pawns. The c3 pawn supports d4 but the c-pawn structure is permane.
plans: Target the doubled c-pawns in the middlegame and endgame
Use the open b-file for rook pressure
alternatives: Nb6 — retreating, but this is too passive and wastes the whole point of the G...

moveAnnotations.annotation: White recaptures bxc3, maintaining the center but accepting doubled c-pawns. The c3 pawn supports d4 directly, and the center remains imposing. But the doubled pawns are a permanent structural weakness that Black will target in the endgame.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White: e4, d4, c3 — a powerful central chain.
plans: Push the center pawns forward for an attack
Use the bishop pair in the open position
alternatives: Bxc3 — very rare, gives up the bishop pair for a cleaner structure

moveAnnotations.annotation: The bishop takes its throne on g7, and now the Grünfeld's strategy is fully revealed. The Bg7 stares down the long a1-h8 diagonal directly at the d4 pawn — the center that White spent four moves building is now under heavy fire from Black's most powerful piece.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No change; the bishop enhances Black's pressure on the dark squares and the d4 pawn.
plans: Combine Bg7 pressure with c5 to attack d4
Castle and bring rooks to open files
alternatives: c5 — immediately attacking d4 before completing the fianchetto

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to f3, supporting d4 and preparing to castle. The Nf3 is well-placed — it defends the center, controls e5, and prepares to complete development.
plans: Castle kingside and start a central or kingside advance
Use Bc4 or Bd3 to develop the light-squared bishop actively
alternatives: Bc4 — developing the bishop first, eyeing f7

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays c5, the critical central break that strikes at d4 — the base of White's center. With the Bg7 pressuring d4 from the diagonal and ...c5 attacking it with a pawn, White's center is under enormous strain.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black's c5 creates direct tension against d4. The Grunfeld's central battle is now fully engaged.
plans: Increase pressure on d4 after dxc5 or if White maintains the center
Open the c-file for rook activity
alternatives: O-O — castling first before the central break

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the bishop to e3, adding a third defender to the d4 pawn (alongside Nf3 and the c3 pawn). The Be3 also controls the a7-g1 diagonal and prepares Qd2 to create a queen+bishop battery.
plans: Solidify d4 and maintain the center
Prepare Qd2 and potentially castle queenside for aggressive play
alternatives: Be2 — a quieter developing move preparing to castle kingside

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays Qa5, pinning the c3 pawn and adding another attacker to White's structure. The Qa5 pressures c3 and a2, and in some lines can swing to a4 or c7. The queen is actively placed on the queenside where Black's counterplay is focused.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: No change, but the queen on a5 makes it harder for White to hold c3 and d4 simultaneously.
plans: Win the c3 pawn or force White into an awkward defense
Combine queen pressure with Bg4 or Nc6 to pile on d4
alternatives: O-O — castling first for safety

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays Qd2, creating a battery with the Be3 and connecting the rooks. The Qd2 defends c3, supports O-O-O in some lines, and keeps the position together under Black's queenside pressure.
plans: Castle queenside and launch a kingside pawn storm
Maintain the center and use the bishop pair
alternatives: Rc1 — defending c3 with the rook instead

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles, getting the king safe before intensifying the central pressure. The Rf8 is now available for ...Rd8 (pressuring d4) or ...f5 (a kingside break in some lines).
plans: Launch queenside counterplay against White's king
Activate the rooks on c8, d8, or b8
alternatives: Nc6 — developing a piece before castling to increase central pressure

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays Rc1, defending c3 with the rook and preparing to hold the queenside together. The Rc1 supports the c3 pawn, eyes the c-file, and keeps White's structure solid under pressure.
plans: Castle queenside with a solid defensive setup
Use the c-file for potential counterplay
alternatives: O-O-O — castling immediately

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black captures cxd4, the critical exchange that challenges White's center directly. After cxd4 (or exd4), the pawn structure is transformed and the middlegame battle over the center's remnants begins in earnest.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: After cxd4 cxd4, White has an isolated d4 pawn — dynamically strong but a long-term target.
plans: Blockade and target the isolated d4 pawn
Use the open c-file and long diagonal for piece activity
alternatives: Nc6 — maintaining the tension and developing another piece

moveAnnotations.annotation: White opens with the queen's pawn, immediately claiming central space and preparing to build a classical pawn center.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White establishes a central pawn on d4, controlling key central squares and preparing for potentia.
plans: Develop pieces toward the center
Prepare e4 to build a strong pawn center

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to its ideal square, immediately putting pressure on White's central pawn.
plans: Challenge White's center with pieces
Prepare kingside development and castling

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays the English Opening move, controlling the crucial d5 square and preparing to build a broad pawn center. This move supports a future e4 advance and begins the Queen's Gambit structure.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White creates a pawn chain on c4-d4, controlling central light squares and preparing for e4.
plans: Build the ideal pawn center with e4
Control the d5 square to limit Black's central options

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black signals the Grünfeld Defense with this characteristic fianchetto move. Rather than defending passively, Black prepares to place the bishop on g7 where it will eye the long diagonal and White's.
plans: Fianchetto the bishop to g7 for long diagonal pressure
Attack White's pawn center from a distance

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to its most active square, supporting the d4 pawn and preparing the advance e4. From c3, the knight controls important central squares and will support White's pawn storm.
plans: Support the advance e4 for a strong pawn center
Be ready to recapture on d5 after Black's pawn break

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strikes at the heart of White's position with the signature Grünfeld pawn break! This courageous move challenges White's c4 pawn and opens the center, even though it appears to give White a po.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black challenges White's pawn chain with d5, creating immediate central tension and potential pawn.
plans: Trade pawns to open lines for piece activity
Use the fianchettoed bishop to pressure White's center

moveAnnotations.annotation: White accepts the challenge and captures the pawn, opening the c-file and maintaining central tension.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: The pawn exchange opens the center and the c-file, creating a more complex pawn structure.
plans: Recapture to maintain material balance
Use the open c-file for piece activity

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black recaptures with the knight, centralizing it powerfully on d5. From this excellent square, the knight controls many important squares and puts immediate pressure on White's position.
plans: Centralize the knight before it gets kicked out
Prepare to deal with White's e4 advance

moveAnnotations.annotation: White advances the e-pawn with tempo, attacking Black's centralized knight and establishing the famous pawn center.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White creates the powerful d4-e4 pawn center, controlling key central squares and gaining space ad.
plans: Force the knight to move and maintain the strong center
Develop pieces to support the pawn center

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black's knight captures the c3 knight, the key trade that defines the Exchange Variation.
plans: Trade pieces to reduce White's attacking potential
Follow up with moves like ...Bg7 and ...c5 to pressure the center

moveAnnotations.annotation: White recaptures with the b-pawn, accepting doubled c-pawns in exchange for maintaining the powerful pawn center.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White now has doubled c-pawns but maintains the strong d4-e4 center.
plans: Use the strong pawn center to launch an attack
Develop pieces quickly to support the center

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black completes the fianchetto, placing the bishop on the long diagonal where it exerts maximum pressure on White's center.
plans: Pressure White's center along the long diagonal
Support the upcoming ...c5 pawn break

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to its ideal square, supporting the e4 pawn and preparing to castle kingside.

Support the central pawns with piece development

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strikes with the signature Grünfeld counterattack! This pawn break directly challenges White's d4 pawn and opens the c-file, beginning serious pressure against White's center.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black creates immediate tension in the center with c5, challenging White's pawn chain and opening.
plans: Continue pressuring White's center with pieces

Black develops the knight, a standard response.

White pushes e4, building an imposing pawn center. This is the Exchange Variation.
Black exchanges the knight, doubling White's c-pawns? No — White recaptures with the b-pawn, gaining a massive center.
White's center is impressive: pawns on c3, d4, e4. But Black will target them with the Bg7 and ...c5.

White blocks the check with the bishop, which also develops a piece.
Black grabs the a2 pawn! This is a critical decision — Black is material up but the queen is somewhat out of play.
